top.value: The project focuses on immediate relief with two main pillars: WASH and Multipurpose Cash Transfer. It includes providing drinking water, repairing water sources, and installing emergency sanitation facilities. Additionally, it entails giving a one-time cash distribution to registered IDP households to address urgent needs for water, sanitation, and purchasing power.

Une réponse précoce est déployée pour aider les ménages vulnérables affectés par les inondations/cyclones dans la Région Atsimo Atsinanana, via des distributions monétaires inconditionnelles et conditionnelles, leur permettant de subvenir à leurs besoins alimentaires jusqu'à la reprise de leurs activités habituelles. Les objectifs comprennent des transferts monétaires, des communications et des sensibilisations pour promouvoir des changements de comportement selon le principe IEC.

The overall impact of the proposed action is the containment of the spread of COVID-19 in the targeted communities and the decrease and prevention of disease and malnutrition, including the decrease of loss of livelihoods and human assets. The outcomes we aim for is that target communities have taken the appropriate measures to stop COVID-19 transmission within the community and that the overall food and nutrition security of the target groups is not further impacted by the COVID-19 crisis. This will be achieved through access to humanitarian assistance across multiple response sectors, and protection services for human assets, social cohesion, rights and livelihoods enhancement.

A three-year Africa-wide program to mitigate humanitarian crises in seven countries of Sub-Saharan Africa – Burundi, Central African Republic, Ethiopia, Kenya, Niger, Somalia, South Sudan - is proposed here. The aim of the project is to ensure the basic provision of sufficient drinking water and access to safe sanitation and hygiene services as well as the provision of needs-based emergency aid to prevent or decrease morbidities, mortality and acute malnutrition especially for vulnerable people in acute humanitarian crises triggered by flight and displacement, droughts, floods and anticipated periods of hunger.

Enhance food security in India, Malawi, Nepal, and Uganda through scalable digital innovations. The Project aim to digital literacy, scaling quality data collection to train AI for the Child Growth Monitor app, expand access of farmers to Digital Agriculture Solutions through leveraging synergies across technology advancements, AI, collaborative content creation, and business model testing, as well as facilitating cross-country exchange sessions. The project aims to reach 13,050 children under the age of 6 yr, 460,000 smallholder farmers through our implementing partners: ACE, FUM, CSONA, PALM, Li-Bird and DSC

Objective: Contribute to enhanced food security and nutrition in India, Nepal, Malawi, and Uganda through advanced digital innovations that are scalable, sustainable, and integrated into in-country systems. Outcome: Improved food security, enhanced nutrition outcomes, and sustainable economic development are achieved by effectively scaling and integrating digital innovations into national systems and communities. These advancements are facilitated through the further development of digital agriculture solutions, the expansion of digital literacy, improved data-driven health interventions via the Child Growth Monitor (CGM), and the establishment of social enterprises. A total of 483,000  project participants will be involved, ensuring broad and inclusive impact across the target regions.

To reduce effects of disasters on local communities in eight countries of intervention 40 local and national humanitarian organisations (five per country) and four international consortium partners will jointly develop and undertake capacity strengthening and exchange measures in order to reinforce their institutional and operational capacity for an effective and timely response to humanitarian crises and localised, principled humanitarian action. The ToGETHER programme promotes complementary partnerships and advocates for the local partners’ roles as leading local actors in humanitarian response, putting localisation - understood as transformation - into practice.

The project develops and establishes an Anticipatory Humanitarian Action Mechanism (Forecast-based Action/FbA) to address imminent risks of food insecurity in Madagascar, Zimbabwe and Kenya. The mechanism reduces expected impacts of drought hazards on vulnerable communities through the implementation of preventive Early Actions. Key elements are the scientific development of drought risk models, identification of trigger levels, risk and vulnerability analyses and the development of Early Action Protocols (EAPs).Scale-up and extension of the existing FbA mechanism in MadagascarReplication of the FbA mechanism in Zimbabwe and KenyaRespective EAPs are then linked to a Trigger-based Finance Facility to ensure proper financial coverage

The project's interventions will directly engage children, health workers, caregivers, small-scale and medium-scale farmers, with a deliberate focus on women, youth, refugees, host communities, and individuals with less literacy skills. These participants will benefit from a range of tools and activities, including the Child Growth Monitor, agricultural extension apps (AgriApps), digital literacy clinics, and support for in-country entrepreneurs and community-based organizations. Project participants have been selected based on the operational regions of our implementing partners to ensure relevance and impact. Indirectly, the project is expected to positively affect an estimated 483,000 individuals, encompassing household members of direct participants and those who will benefit from the broader ripple effects of the project’s initiatives.

Les réfugiés, les rapatriés, les déplacés internes et les communautés d'accueil affectés par la violence, le changement climatique et/ou Covid-19 au Mali, au Niger et au Burkina Faso peuvent couvrir leurs besoins primaires de base en matière d'alimentation, de nutrition, d'hygiène et d'abri dans un environnement protégé.

Since the Ebola crisis, everyone in Sierra Leone now recognises that better hygiene and a healthier environment are essential to prevent more epidemics. Unauthorised dumping sites and illegal waste disposal spread illnesses, so Welthungerhilfe is helping the government to develop an infrastructure for municipal waste collection. Sierra Leone's private sector is underdeveloped so it is expected to participate in providing public services and is also involved in this project. At the same time, jobs are being created for the country’s numerous unemployed young people. Awareness of waste reduction, separation and recycling is also being raised by various means such as through schools.
Germany - Ministry for Economic Cooperation and Development

The project focuses on immediate relief with two main pillars: WASH and Multipurpose Cash Transfer. It includes providing drinking water, repairing water sources, and installing emergency sanitation facilities. Additionally, it entails giving a one-time cash distribution to registered IDP households to address urgent needs for water, sanitation, and purchasing power.

1. Provide access to immediate life-saving WASH assistance (emergency response and early recovery) to flood-affected target communities in selected districts.2. Provide access to comprehensive Mental Health and Psychosocial Support Services (MHPSS) and quality GBV care (in line with global IASC guidelines on MHPSS in humanitarian emergency settings)  to flood affected population in targeted districts.
Multi-purpose cash grants, cash for work/trainings and transport vouchers to access health services and shelter) is provided to the most vulnerable HHs affected by the floods (emergency response)
